title: Who is the Best Masters Winner?
text:
“A tradition unlike any other.” When April rolls around, the immortal words of Jim Nantz, first coined in 1986, ring ever more true.
The green jackets, the joy, the heart break, the Pimento Cheese sandwiches. These traditions all make the weekend at Augusta National Golf Club all the more memorable. Unlike all other Majors in golf, the Masters is the only one that is played at the same place every single year: Augusta, Georgia.
First held in 1934, and every Sunday since (except 1943-1945 for a certain world conflict), we’ve tuned in to see the green jacket placed on the years’ Masters winner. There have been 56 different winners, with some golfers even winning multiple green jackets.
As we turn our sights to Sunday, though, I was faced with an interesting question: who’s the best? I mean, yes, they all are: they’ve won the Masters, which is much better than I have done… so far.
But it is nonetheless an interesting question. If you put them all into a round together, who are the names that might come out on top and score another victory at Augusta? That’s what I aim to find out.
For this, we’ll be looking at all Masters winners from 1997 to 2025. Why start at 1997? Well, a certain golfer named after a certain large striped cat won his first jacket that year, but we’ll get to him later.

Green Jackets, Multiple
Before we begin, it’s important to note that, while there have been 56 winners of the Masters, 18 of these have been won multiple times by the same people, but only 4 have won more than once since 1997.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggbump)
library(gt)

masters <- read_csv("Master's Winners New - Sheet1 copy.csv")

winners <- masters |>
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Scottie Scheffler" |
    PLAYER == "Bubba Watson" |
    PLAYER == "Phil Mickelson" |
    PLAYER == "Tiger Woods"
  )

multiple <- winners |> 
  select(PLAYER, `TOTAL SCORE`, `TOTAL PAR`, YEAR)

multiple |> 
  gt() |> 
  tab_header(
    title = "Who Has Multiple Green Jackets in Their Locker?",
    subtitle = "Augusta can be unforgiving. However, there are 4 men who, since 1997, were lucky enough to leave Masters Sunday as a champion multiple times."
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**By:** Brian Streeter  |  **Source:** The Masters")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none")








Who Has Multiple Green Jackets in Their Locker?


Augusta can be unforgiving. However, there are 4 men who, since 1997, were lucky enough to leave Masters Sunday as a champion multiple times.

By: Brian Streeter | Source: The Masters










The Tournament
At Augusta National, during the Masters, golfers play through 4 rounds of golf, with half of the players being cut after Round 2. Though they’re still playing the same course, scores can change drastically. For example, this year, Augusta has had the lowest amount of rain on record. So, because of the dry grass, the greens are much faster than normal. But, what if it rains overnight? The greens become a lot slower.
So, what do our winners’ scores look like from round to round?


Code
masters <- read_csv("Master's Winners New - Sheet1 copy.csv")


masterslong <- masters |> 
  mutate(playeryear = paste(PLAYER, YEAR)) |> 
  select(playeryear, R1, R2, R3, R4) |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols=-playeryear, 
    names_to = "Round",
    values_to = "Strokes"
  )

roryround <- masterslong |> 
  filter(
   playeryear == "Rory McIlroy 2025" 
  ) 


scottie22round <- masterslong |> 
  filter(
   playeryear == "Scottie Scheffler 2022" 
  )

tiger19round <- masterslong |> 
  filter(
   playeryear == "Tiger Woods 2019" 
  )

phil06round <- masterslong |> 
  filter(
   playeryear == "Phil Mickelson 2006" 
  )

tiger97round <- masterslong |> 
  filter(
   playeryear == "Tiger Woods 1997" 
  )

ggplot() + 
  geom_bump(data=masterslong, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear), color = "lightgrey")+
  geom_point(data=masterslong, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear), color = "lightgrey", size = 4) +
  geom_bump(data=roryround, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color = "Rory McIlroy, 2025"))+
   geom_point(data=roryround, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color ="Rory McIlroy, 2025"), size = 4)+
geom_bump(data=scottie22round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color = "Scottie Scheffler, 2022"))+
   geom_point(data=scottie22round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color ="Scottie Scheffler, 2022"), size = 4)+ 
  geom_bump(data=tiger19round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color = "Tiger Woods, 2019"))+
   geom_point(data=tiger19round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color ="Tiger Woods, 2019"), size = 4)+ 
  geom_bump(data=tiger97round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color = "Tiger Woods, 1997"))+
   geom_point(data=tiger97round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color ="Tiger Woods, 1997"), size = 4)+
  geom_bump(data=phil06round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color = "Phil Mickelson, 2006"))+
   geom_point(data=phil06round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color ="Phil Mickelson, 2006"), size = 4)+
  scale_color_manual(
    name = "Golfer",
    values = c(
    "#00563F", # Rory McIlroy, 2025
    "#F99ABA", # Scottie Scheffler, 2022
    "#CFA94A", # Tiger Woods, 1997
    "#C7EA46", # Tiger Woods, 2019
    "#48AB51" # Phil Mickelson, 2006
  ))+
   labs(title="Round by Round, Stroke by Stroke", subtitle="Played over 4 days, the Masters can be unpredictable, and it can break hearts. It also requires \nconsistency. So, how do our winners' scores look round by round?", caption="Source: The Masters | By Brian Streeter") +
  theme_minimal()











Round 4: “Hello, Friends”
This is it. The final day of the Masters. By the end of this day, one golfer will come out on top. They’ll put on the highly coveted green jacket, and hoist the trophy above their head in pure triumph.
Sunday is what makes the Masters so memorable. The towering Georgia Pines, the radiant Azaleas, the pristine fairways. Only the best of the best make it to Sunday, and only the best wins it all.
But, in order to do this - they have to get the lowest score - when all eyes are on them, alone.
So, when the pressure is at an all time high, who kept their score at an all time low?


Code
winners <- read_csv("Master's Winners New - Sheet1 copy.csv")

round4 <- winners |> 
  mutate(playeryear = paste(PLAYER, YEAR)) 

rory <-round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Rory McIlroy"
  )

tiger <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Tiger Woods"
  )

scottie <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Scottie Scheffler"
  )

jordan <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Jordan Spieth"
  )

phil <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Phil Mickelson"
  )

rahm <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Jon Rahm"
  )

dustin <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Dustin Johnson"
  )

bubba <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Bubba Watson"
  )

ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=round4, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="lightgrey")+
  geom_bar(data=rory, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill = "#9BCE6F")+
  geom_bar(data=tiger, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#7dc044")+
  geom_bar(data=scottie, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#00563F")+
  geom_bar(data=phil, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#17240c")+
  geom_bar(data=rahm, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#CEE7B8")+
  geom_bar(data=jordan, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#CFA94A")+
  geom_bar(data=dustin, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="lightgreen")+
   geom_bar(data=bubba, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#F99ABA")+
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()+ 
  labs(
    x="Masters Winner", 
    y="Strokes", 
    title="Round 4: I Want a Sunday Kind of Love", 
    subtitle="Here we are. The Final Day of the Masters. Golfers that make it to Round 4 need to keep their scores low. \nSo, who was able to pack a new green jacket in their suitcase home?", 
    caption="Source: The Masters | By Brian Streeter"
    )+
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 











The Verdict?
So, who is the best Masters winner?
Tiger Woods.
Is there really any question? Tiger has won 5 individual Masters championships, second only to Jack Nicklaus with 6. (‘63, ‘65, ‘66, ‘72, ‘75, ‘86)
Tiger has been a staple of the game, and made it what it is today.
